On the matter of “ fictives,” or members of systems who share identities with characters from fictional media, my hypothesis is that their identities are subconscious psychological connections to those characters. I don’t believe that the characters literally jumped from their “source” to someone else’s head; I think that it’s a matter of identity formation that ties someone’s consciousness to that source.

It depends on how they 'came to be'. Some fictives /soulbonds are thought-created by a person who feels a strong connection to a character they may have watched or read about. Hence, bringing them to life as a result within their mind-space. For others, a connection may also have been made, but instead of simply bringing them to life, they may connect to the place where they live. There are variations on how this takes place and how long until the person arrives completely.

Aye, the Chicopee, a fine-un, she were. Clean built and trig-lookin’! None more fleet in ‘64 than she...
fleet Soil, and that 'tis mixed with a great quantity of Earth, Marle, Mud or Clay, &c.

[…] it was not till the afternoon that they came out on the high-road, their first high-road; and there disaster, fleet and unforeseen, sprang out on them — disaster momentous indeed to their expedition […]

definitions: (US, rail transport) An automatic operation mode of an absolute signal that reserves a route for several trains following one another, without the need for dispatcher to re-set the route for each train.
present participle and gerund of fleet
Passing quickly; of short duration.
That which flees, especially quickly; fugitive.
examples: During the fleeting summer months of his field season, when the outer vestiges of winter melted briefly, there were ponds and pools and lakes of water everywhere.
For starters, we see examples all the time of some middle-aged men trying to hang onto their own fleeting youth by sporting younger women on their arms.

As they passed, accelerating, on to the bridge and felt the first bite of the incline beyond, one had a fleeting glimpse of driver and fireman, illumined as by the fires of hell, the one tugging at the regulator handle, the other shovelling for dear life.

Architecture, sculpture, painting are static arts. Even in literature "our flying minds," as George Meredith says, cannot contain protracted description. It is so; for from sequences of words they must assemble all the details in one simultaneous impression. But moments of fleeting beauty too transient to be caught by any means less swift than light itself are registered on the screen.

definitions: The quality of being fleeting.
examples: But the perpetual mutability and fleetingness of those immediate objects of sight render them incapable of being managed after the manner of geometrical figures […]

definitions: In a fleet manner; swiftly
The quality of being fleet or swift; rapidity.
present participle and gerund of fleme
(nautical) Ellipsis of Flemish coil (“ a rope that has been arranged into a neat, flat spiral coil ”).
examples: A coiled, faked, or flemished down line is said to be laid up in coils, fakes, or flemishes.

Frequently fancy Flemishes are made, for example, with a regular Flemish coil in the center and the rest of the line placed in various shapes about it but always so as to retain the appearance of a mat.

definitions: (transitive, nautical) Often followed by down: to arrange (a rope) into a neat, flat spiral coil.
examples: The two Swedish sailors on deck, lounging and spitting into the water, seemed not to have been kept up to their duties, for paint work and bright work were far from brilliant, and the ropes were badly pointed and flemished.

In about twenty minutes, after the messenger had been stowed away, the cables coiled in the tiers, and the ropes flemished down on deck, the captain made his appearance, and directed the first-lieutenant to send aft the newly impressed men.

When great neatness is desired a line is flemished down. Successive circles of the line are wrapped about each other with the free end at the center. When it is finished it looks like a mat and with an old piece of line can be used as one. […] If a line is flemished down and left on a deck for some time it will mark the deck as well as remain wet on the under side, and therefore deteriorate. On small boats lines are usually either coiled down or flemished down.

definitions: To strip the blubber or skin from, as from a whale, seal, etc.
examples: His eyes sprang open. Umegat stared straight at him for the fraction of a second, and Cazaril felt flensed.
It engulfed screaming soldiers who disappeared before his eyes, their flesh, armor, even bone, flensed into a suspended mist that was heading straight for them.
In this domain right sex is capital, it flenses the feelings of all the poisonous artifices brought in by the think-box in the guise of clever ideas.

For that reason, among others, he would never evince the particular guantness, the cut and flagrant sense of purpose - all compromise and capacity for surrender flensed away - which had made Thomas Covenant ir-refusable to her.

The Lemakot in the north strangled widows and threw them into the cremation pyres of their dead husbands. If they defeated potential invaders the New Irish hanged the vanquished from banyan trees, flensed their windpipes, removed their heads, left their intestines to jerk in the sun.

definitions: The skin of a human or animal.
Animal tissue regarded as food; meat (but sometimes excluding fish).
The soft tissue of the body, especially muscle and fat.
The human body as a physical entity.
The soft, often edible, parts of fruits or vegetables.
A yellowish pink color; the color of some Caucasian human skin.
examples: He is our brother and our flesh.
Chicken is already the most popular meat in the US, and is projected to be the planet’s favourite flesh by 2020.
The flesh of black walnuts was a protein-packed winter food carefully hoarded in tall, stilted buildings.
But death had no gift for me, no power to free me from flesh.
There is no flesh in man's obdurate heart.

definitions: To bury (something, especially a weapon) in flesh.
To glut.
To reward (a hound, bird of prey etc.) with flesh of the animal killed, to excite it for further hunting; to train (an animal) to have an appetite for flesh.
To put flesh on; to fatten.
To remove the flesh from the skin during the making of leather.
examples: Give me a clean sword and a clean foe to flesh it in.
Before they had fleshed the hounds, however, he recollected himself […].

definitions: simple past and past participle of flesh
(in combination) Having a specified form of flesh or body.
Having flesh; corpulent.
The state or characteristic of being fleshy.
examples: The truth is, the Frenchman is rather inclined to fleshiness; yet the soup-meagre theory still prevails amongst the mass of Englishmen.

[T]he fleshiness of the chin, and the jovial redundancy of the cheeks, were, in their turn, utterly at variance with the character of the pale, noble forehead, and the expression of the quick, intelligent eyes.

definitions: Of or relating to non- spiritual or non- religious matters.
Of or relating to the body.
Of, relating to or resembling flesh; composed of flesh; having a lot of flesh.
Of or relating to pleasurable (often sexual) sensations.
examples: When from their fleshly Bondage they are free, Then what divine, and future things they see!
those inward pains that agonize us more than all our fleshly wounds
[…] abstain from fleshly lusts, which war against the soul.
[…] lay on load enough vpon e’m, and spare e’m not, for the’re good plump fleshly Asses, and may well enough beare it:
[…] in the body of this fleshly land, This kingdom, this confine of blood and breath, Hostility and civil tumult reigns Between my conscience and my cousin’s death.

definitions: Of, related to, or resembling flesh.
(of a person) Having considerable flesh; plump.
(wine) Rich and smooth, with significant body or texture.
examples: The King's face was slightly more fleshy than mine, the oval of its contour the least trifle more pronounced.
He was a large fleshy man, weighing at least two hundred pounds, and he quickly became a faithful representation of a quivering jelly-mountain of fat.
[O]ver reefs and banks of shining rock, a bristling beard of spiky and fleshy vegetation was straining into view.
Mr. Creakle... showed me the cane, and asked me what I thought of THAT?... Did it bite? At every question he gave me a fleshy cut with it that made me writhe.

It's hard to imagine why Flemish Renaissance artist Peter Paul Rubens would paint a blemish on the backside of one of the fleshy lovelies meant to represent beauty, charm and good cheer, but there's no denying that single red brushstroke in the midst of his central figure's creamy skin.

definitions: Generally, a manufacturer of bows and arrows.
A device to assist in fletching or feathering arrows.
One who fletches or feathers arrows.
examples: An arrow maker is a fletcher, or, more exactly, one who feathers arrows.
A fletcher is the tool that actually holds the arrow and sets the vane or feather in place while it is being glued.
I don't like using a fletcher, the single feather ones are too slow, and the three feather ones inaccurate.
Very speedily the Fletcher arrived. It was his trade, the making of bows and arrows, and the marks of the various makers were as well known to him as the marks on his own.
They [some boards] had been washed away from the fletcher by the flood.

definitions: (countable, slang) An act or instance of flaunting something; a boast or brag; something considered impressive.
A flexible insulated electrical cord.
Flexibility, pliancy.
(uncountable, chiefly UK, Ireland) Any flexible insulated electrical wiring.
An act of flexing.
Flexible ductwork, typically flexible plastic over a metal wire coil to shape a tube.
(countable, geometry) A point of inflection.
examples: It's an achievement to stand out from other Marvel movies in terms of special effects, but this whole movie feels like a flex for those computer wizards.

Flex is quick and cheaper to install than metallic systems, but it yields higher pressure loss than other types of ducts and requires runs of less than 15 feet, minimal turns and elimination of kinks.

The books lent her by Dr Grimble on period furniture and ceramics interested her deeply, and she studied them at night in the silence of the shop, where she had a flex and a reading lamp installed at her own expense.

Getting together with other power players at Masa is the ultimate flex of conspicuous consumption. […] A party of five or more requires a deposit of $200 per person at least one week prior to the reservation.

The hills become more rounded. The slopes are either the stooped shoulders of an aging colossus or the muscular flexes of a geologic youngster, but they are pleasant, comforting. This landscape is what most would think of […]

definitions: To move part of the body using one's muscles.
(intransitive, slang) To flaunt one's superiority; to show off.
To tighten the muscles for display of size or strength.
(transitive, slang) To boast or brag about; to flaunt (something).
(transitive, chiefly physics or biomechanics) To bend something.
To repeatedly bend one of one's joints.
examples: The board flex es as you step on it.
I've got billions and billions of rhymes to flex / 'Cause I've got more rhymes than Carl Sagan's got turtlenecks
They say it's lonely at the top, but this the best shit ever / Hey, don't you see me out here shinin'? Bitch I'm barely flexin'

I shook my head, wondering if I was gonna have to knock Pimp on his ass. "I don't know where," I flexed on him with my height advantage, "you getting all this 'soft' bullshit from, dawg, but you just keep fuckin around. I can show you gangsta better than I can tell you."

He rubbed his hands together. "Believe it or not, there was a time when I considered giving acting a go. What do you think, Miss Fox?" He flexed impressive biceps. "Would I have had a chance against the Schwarzeneggers and the Chuck Norris types?"

definitions: The quality of being flexible, whether physically or metaphorically.
The quality of having options.
examples: I had some flexibility in terms of whether to stay in a hotel or in a bed-and-breakfast.
Researchers have developed a new gel they say is as durable as metal, has the flexibility of jello, and could revolutionize how our bodies heal and age.
Whether redundancies come and whether they result in industrial action remains to be seen, but it's clear that the RMT is not prepared to show any flexibility towards rail companies.

definitions: (chiefly engineering and manufacturing) Something that is flexible.
examples: Alcan is mostly flexibles -- and so it boosts Amcor's flexible packaging business to a globally significant $7 billion one.

definitions: Capable of adapting or changing to suit new or modified conditions or situations.
Willing or prone to give way to the influence of others; not invincibly rigid or obstinate.
Capable or being adapted or molded in some way.
Capable of being flexed or bent without breaking; able to be turned or twisted without breaking.
examples: When the splitting wind Makes flexible the knees of knotted oaks.
You can't always get what you want: you need to learn to be flexible.
Women are soft, mild, pitiful, and flexible.
Phocion the Athenian (a man of great severity, and no ways flexible to the will of the people […]
a flexible language

definitions: The state or quality of being flexible.
In a flexible manner.
examples: But while the decline in season ticket sales as a proportion of fares income has continued, commuting has by no means died. Instead, people are working more flexibly. Therefore, a monthly or annual season ticket is not worthwhile.

definitions: Flexible; capable of being repeatedly flexed without breaking.
examples: Rooted in earth each cloven hoof descends, / And round and round her flexile neck she bends […].

definitions: The act of bending a joint, especially a bone joint; the counteraction of extension.
Deviation from straightness.
The state of being bent or flexed.
A muscle whose contraction acts to bend a joint or limb.
examples: Starting at the point of shoulder, use muscle squeezings, picking-ups, kneadings and gentle frictions, interspersed with effleurages, over the triceps muscle as well as the fleshy part of the flexor and extensor muscle groups.

definitions: winding from side to side; sinuous
(zoology) The last joint, or bend, of the wing of a bird.
A turn; a bend; a fold; a curve.
The act of bending or flexing; flexion.

(astronomy) The small distortion of an astronomical instrument caused by the weight of its parts; the amount to be added or subtracted from the observed readings of the instrument to correct them for this distortion.

examples: varying with the flexures of the valley through which it meandered

The Stomach [of a weasel] about three inches long; proportionably, more than a Dogs. An inch in Diametre at the upper Orifice; and the nether, ¼; having a flexure towards its Conjunction with the Guts: ſhaped like to the body of a pair of Bag-Pipes.

definitions: To introduce a flexure into; to bend or flex.
An offbeat, skittish person; especially said of a young woman.

examples: All this responsibility at such an early age made her a bitchy flibbertigibbet.

“Thou flibbertigibbet,” cried Catwezle, who had been listening, “all is clear as springwater. This Rapkyn was a sorcerer!”

definitions: (tennis) A powerful underarm volley shot.
(fencing) A cut that lands with the point, often involving a whip of the foible of the blade to strike at a concealed target.
A flitch.
(informal) A motion picture, movie, film; (in plural, usually preceded by "the") movie theater, cinema.

definitions: To pass by rapidly, so as not to be perceived clearly.
To move or hit (something) with a short, quick motion.
examples: They were quiet for a time, watching the fields and lanes flick past.
to flick the dirt from boots
flick one's hair
Near-synonym: flit

definitions: (US) Any of certain small woodpeckers, especially of the genus Colaptes.
(figurative) A short moment.

the flicker of an old movie
the flicker of the dying candle
a flicker of emotion
a flicker of hope

definitions: To burn or shine unsteadily, or with a wavering light.
To flutter or flap.
To keep going on and off; to appear and disappear for short moments; to flutter.
examples: The shadows flicker to and fro.
A strong desire to smash up everything in the room kept flickering in his head.
And flickering on her nest made short essays to sing.

Long after his cigar burnt bitter, he sat with eyes fixed on the blaze. When the flames at last began to flicker and subside, his lids fluttered, then drooped; but he had lost all reckoning of time when he opened them again to find Miss Erroll in furs and ball-gown kneeling on the hearth and heaping kindling on the coals, […]

definitions: A short, uncertain burst.
examples: Even after the acrimonious breakup, she still felt flickerings of love for him.

definitions: present participle and gerund of flicker
shining unsteadily or varying rapidly in brightness (referring to a light or a source of light)
examples: With 3,600 h.p. underfoot, acceleration was reasonably brisk, but the flickering wheel-slip indicator light showed the prudence of not putting full power through the traction motors while there were traces of early-morning dampness on the rails.

definitions: The North American ground squirrel, Citellus richardsoni.
comparative form of fly: more fly

plural of fly
examples: Hung from the “ flies ” in air, She acts a palpable lie, She’s as little a fairy there As unpoetical I!

definitions: third-person singular simple present indicative of fly
A trip made by an aircraft, particularly one between two cities or countries, which is often planned or reserved in advance.
The act of fleeing.
A floor which is reached by stairs or escalators.

A group of canal locks with a short distance between them.

definitions: (sports, by extension, transitive) To throw or kick something so as to send it flying with more loft or airtime than usual.
(cricket, of a spin bowler) To throw the ball in such a way that it has more airtime and more spin than usual.
examples: Riyad Mahrez flighted the free-kick that followed to the far post and Morgan, with not much finesse but plenty of desire, bundled the ball over the line. Cue pandemonium in the stands.

definitions: simple past and past participle of flight
(of birds) Having flight feathers (for example, upon reaching maturity).

(of birds) Capable of flight.
examples: In comparison with flighted birds, the forelimb of a flightless bird typically has a substantially smaller carpometacarpus and ulna-radius, and the relative proportions between these midwing elements and the humerus, and of these last two to […]

definitions: The quality of being flighty.
Unable to fly.

Oh great, some wind is blowing in Chicago, so now I'm flightless in Seattle, because this airline can't cope!

definitions: Given to unplanned and silly ideas or actions.
(of a bird) That flies easily or often.
examples: Permanent way and other checks then somewhat slowed down this flighty progress.

"We know that flighty birds suffer from greater risk of raptor predation, and I assume that it is useful to understand the effects of urbanization because flighty species may be more vulnerable to anthropogenic disturbance."

definitions: To swindle or cheat.
In a flimsy manner
examples: They had barely time to regain the garden, when the flames shot through the house from top to bottom; for it was flimsily built inside and as dry as tinder.

definitions: The quality of being flimsy.
A thing which is ill-founded, unconvincing, or weak.
Thin typing paper used together with carbon paper in a typewriter to make multiple copies of a document; (countable) a sheet of such paper.

Is every body incapable of reaſon, and making a right eſtimate of the merits of men? caught vvith mere outſide? chooſing the flimſy before the ſubſtantial?

[Footnotes: †† Served to deceive the unwary. ‡‡ Pocket book.]

definitions: To make (something) likely to be easily damaged.
(figurative) To treat (someone or something) as paltry or unimportant; to demean, to underestimate.
examples: What she sacrificed in energy, emotion and integrity, diminished her rather than excelled. […] Teri suddenly saw herself flimsied by bargains she had negotiated too readily.

Did you, as a matter of fact, receive pages 55 and 56 at the same time?—I cannot say that I did. / But if you had received them?—I should have flimsied them with this. / As they are not flimsied, what do you say?—All I can presume is that they were not there. I should not have separated one paper from the other, and flimsied one and left the other.

Some of them, it seems, have been doing the work of two or three journalists in the House, flimsying and syndicating London Letters and Labour articles and notes in a number of newspapers. Cannot they be peacefully persuaded to attend to their own work, and leave journalism to journalists?

definitions: (figurative)

examples: He expected the flimsy structure to collapse at any moment.
the flimsiest of theories
a flimsy excuse

definitions: A reflexive jerking away.
(croquet) The slipping of the foot from a ball, when attempting to give a tight croquet.
examples: My eye doctor hates the flinch I have every time he tries to get near my eyes.

definitions: To make a sudden, involuntary movement in response to a (usually negative) stimulus; to cringe; to blench.

(croquet) To let the foot slip from a ball, when attempting to give a tight croquet.
examples: A child, by a constant course of kindness, may be accustomed to bear very rough usage without flinching or complaining.

definitions: fragments, splinters
plural of flinder
examples: The heat brought sweat and the sweat blinded. Black flinders gyrated in the fiery air like bats.

definitions: third-person singular simple present indicative of flinder
A tree of the genus Flindersia.
An act of moving the limbs or body with violent movements, especially in a dance.

An act or period of unrestrained indulgence, enthusiasm, or both.
A short romantic, oftentimes sexual, relationship.
An act of throwing, often violently.

definitions: A hard, fine-grained quartz that fractures conchoidally and generates sparks when struck against a material such as steel, because tiny chips of the steel are heated to incandescence and burn in air.

definitions: A bird, the wood stork.

definitions: Of, pertaining to, or resembling a fungus.
examples: He had no hair on the front of his head at all so that the sweep of bone skin, daunting in its fungoid pallor, came right over above his ears.

definitions: Of or pertaining to fungi.

definitions: The flesh of this organism.
(mycology) Any member of the kingdom Fungi; a eukaryotic organism typically having chitin cell walls but no chlorophyll or plastids. Fungi may be unicellular or multicellular.
examples: Plastics are energy-rich substances, which is why many of them burn so readily. Any organism that could unlock and use that energy would do well in the Anthropocene. Terrestrial bacteria and fungi which can manage this trick are already familiar to experts in the field.

definitions: Resembling or characteristic of fungus.

(medicine) Of or pertaining to the umbilical cord.
inflammation of a funiculus, especially of the spermatic cord
(botany) A stalk that connects the seed (or ovule) with the placenta.

(anatomy) Any of several cordlike structures, especially the umbilical cord, or a bundle of nerve fibres (white matter) in the spinal cord. The white matter of the spinal cord is made of (posterior, anterior and lateral) columns/funiculi. The grey columns are also called horns but not funiculi.
examples: The elaiosome emerges from the funiculus.

definitions: Mental depression.

definitions: To envelop with an offensive smell or smoke.

definitions: (US, slang) Not quite right; of questionable quality; not appropriate to the context.

definitions: (marketing, figurative) Ellipsis of purchase funnel (“ the process of customer acquisition conceptualized as a series of stages, from initial awareness (top) to sale or conversion (bottom) ”).
